title: Detecting Bridges in a Spanning Tree: Fix the Edge Traversal

statement.markdown:
Given a graph and a spanning tree, detect bridges in the spanning tree. A bridge is an edge that connects two nodes in a graph and whose removal increases the number of connected components.

You will be given an adjacency list representation of the graph, along with the root of the spanning tree. Your task is to find and return all the edges in the spanning tree that are bridges.
statement.inputFormat: The graph is represented as an adjacency list where each index i contains a list of its adjacent nodes. The spanning tree root is represented as the first node in the graph.
statement.outputFormat: Return a list of edges in the spanning tree that are bridges.
statement.notes: You may assume that the graph is connected and that the spanning tree is a tree. You may also assume that all edge weights are non-negative.

examples.explanation:
The graph has the following spanning tree: 
 1 -> 2 
 2 -> 3 
 3 -> 1 
There is only one bridge in this tree, which is the edge 1-2.

hints.text: Use DFS to traverse the spanning tree and detect bridges.

editorial.title: Detecting Bridges in a Spanning Tree
editorial.intuition: To detect bridges, we can use DFS to traverse the spanning tree. We keep track of the discovery time of each node and the low value of each node. The low value of a node is the smallest discovery time of any node reachable from that node via an edge that has not yet been visited. If an edge has low value less than the discovery time of its end node, then it is a bridge.
editorial.optimalSolution: Use the Tarjan's algorithm to find strongly connected components in the graph. Then use the result to find the bridges in the spanning tree.

functionSignature.python: def findBridges(self, graph, root: int) -> list[int];
